sections.title: What is Mirror
sections.lead: Mirror is a description language for user interfaces. One syntax for structure, styling, and interactions – that compiles to React.

content.text: The best way to learn Mirror is by doing. Every example below is interactive – edit the code, and watch the result update instantly. No setup, no compile step, just immediate feedback.
1. A Component
In Mirror, everything is a component. A component is simply a named piece of UI. Why names? Because names let you reuse things. Define once, use everywhere.

content.code:
Button
    "Hello World"

content.text: That's it. Button is the component name, "Hello World" is what it displays. No boilerplate, no ceremony – just what you want to see.
2. Add Styling
A plain button isn't very exciting. Let's add some style. Properties follow the component name as name value pairs, separated by commas.

content.code:
Row
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12 24
        radius 8
        "Short form"
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12 24
        radius 8
        "Long form"

content.text: Here, background sets the color, padding adds inner spacing, and radius rounds the corners. Notice the two forms: you can write background or just bg. Mirror lets you choose – full words for readability, shortcuts for speed. Both compile to the same result.
3. Reuse and Override

Here's where Mirror gets interesting. Once you style a component, it remembers. Use that component again, and it keeps all its properties. You only specify what's different – no repetition, no copy-paste.

content.code:
Row
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12 24
        radius 8
        "Hello World"
    Button
        "Save"
    Button
        background #c12271
        "Delete"

content.text: Look at the second button – just Button "Save". It inherits the blue background, padding, and radius from the first button automatically. The third button overrides only the background to red. This is how Mirror eliminates redundancy while keeping your code readable.
4. Layouts and Children

Real interfaces aren't flat – they're nested. A card contains a title and a description. A sidebar contains menu items. In Mirror, indentation creates hierarchy. Children are indented under their parent.

content.code:
Tile
    vertical
    background #3281d1
    padding 20
    radius 12
    gap 8
    Label
        font-size 12
        "Revenue"
    Value
        font-size 28
        weight bold
        "2.7 Mio"

content.text: The ver (vertical) property stacks children top-to-bottom. The g (gap) property adds consistent spacing between them. Label and Value are children of Tile – they're indented under it. This is how you build complex layouts from simple pieces.
5. Reusable Components

Now we're ready for the big idea: templates. Add a colon after a component name to define it as a reusable template. The template isn't rendered – it's a blueprint. You define the structure once, then use it many times, filling in different content.

content.code:
Tile:
    vertical
    background #3281d1
    padding 20
    radius 12
    gap 8
    Label:
        font-size 12
    Value:
        font-size 28
        weight bold

Dashboard
    grid 3
    gap 16
    Tile
        Label
            "Revenue"
        Value
            "2.7 Mio"
    Tile
        Label
            "Customers"
        Value
            "40"
    Tile
        Label
            "Growth"
        Value
            "+12%"
    Tile
        Label
            "Users"
        Value
            "1.2k"
    Tile
        Label
            "Orders"
        Value
            "89"
    Tile
        Label
            "Rating"
        Value
            "4.8"

content.text: See how powerful this is? Tile: defines the template with its structure (Label and Value) and styling. Then each Tile usage just fills in the content. Six dashboard tiles, but the styling code exists only once. Change the template, and all six update.
6. States

So far, our button looks the same whether you hover over it or not. But real interfaces respond to interaction. When you hover, something should happen – a color change, a subtle shift. In Mirror, you define these variations as states. The component keeps its default look, and the state block describes what changes.

content.code:
Button
    background #2271c1
    padding 12 24
    radius 8
    "Hover me"
    hover
        background #1d5ba0
    active
        background #174a83

content.text: The hover block kicks in when the mouse hovers over the button. The active block activates when you press it. These are system states – they activate automatically based on user interaction. You just describe what should look different.
7. Click Events

States change appearance, but what about behavior? When the user clicks, something should happen – navigate to a page, show a message, update a value. Events connect user actions to responses. The most common is onclick.

content.code:
Button
    background #2271c1
    padding 12 24
    radius 8
    "Click me"
    hover
        background #1d5ba0
    onclick alert("It works!")

content.text: Simple: onclick followed by an action. Here, alert displays a message. But the real power comes when events control other elements – showing panels, hiding dialogs, switching views.
8. Toggle Visibility

Here's where it gets practical. You can make one element control another. Click a button, and a panel appears. Click again, and it disappears. The toggle action does exactly this – it flips between visible and hidden each time.

content.code:
Box
    vertical
    background #3281d1
    padding 20
    radius 12
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 10 16
        radius 6
        "Show/Hide Details"
        hover
            background #1d5ba0
        onclick toggle Details
    Details
        vertical
        background #222
        padding 16
        radius 8
        gap 4
        Text
            "Here are the details."
        Text
            "They appear and disappear."

content.text: Notice how onclick toggle Details references Details by name. That's how elements talk to each other – by name. No IDs to hunt down, no refs to wire up. Just say which element you want to control.
9. Starting Hidden

Sometimes you want something to start invisible – a tooltip, a dropdown, an error message. The hidden property does exactly this. The element exists in your code, ready to appear, but users don't see it until you reveal it.

content.code:
Box
    vertical
    background #3281d1
    padding 20
    radius 12
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 10 16
        radius 6
        "Show Message"
        hover
            background #1d5ba0
        onclick toggle Message
    Message
        hidden
        "Hello! Click again to hide."

content.text: Message hidden starts invisible. When you click the button, toggle brings it into view. Click again, and it vanishes. This pattern is everywhere: FAQs, accordions, modals, tooltips.
That's Mirror in a nutshell: components, styling, inheritance, states, and events. The following sections explore each concept in depth – with more examples, more patterns, and more power.

sections.title: Components
sections.lead: You've seen that Mirror code is compact. Now let's understand why: everything is a component, and components are defined by usage.

content.text: In most frameworks, you define components separately from using them. Mirror works differently: the first time you use a name, you're also defining it. Write Button with some styling, and every Button after that inherits those styles. It's definition by example.

content.code:
Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 8
        "Next"
    Button
        "Save"
    Button
        "Cancel"
    Button
        "Delete"

content.text: Look at the code: the first Button has all the styling. The next three just have labels. They automatically get the blue background, the padding, and the rounded corners. Why repeat yourself when the system can remember for you?

content.task: Change the color to red (#c14022) and the padding to 12 24.
content.code: Button bg #2271c1, pad 8 16, rad 8, "Click me"

content.text: Definition-by-example is great for quick prototyping. But as your design grows, you might want to define components explicitly – in one place, separate from where you use them. The syntax difference is minimal: just add a colon.

content.code:
Button:
    background #2271c1
    padding 8 16
    radius 8

Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Button
        "Next"
    Button
        "Save"
    Button
        "Cancel"
    Button
        "Delete"

content.text: Button: defines the component without rendering it. It's like saying "this is what a Button looks like" without actually placing one on screen. Later, every Button "Next" uses that definition. This separation makes it easy to move definitions to their own file as your design system grows.

content.task: Define a Card: with dark background and padding, then use it twice.

content.text: What if you want both? Define a component and render it at the same time? The `as` keyword gives you inline definition with immediate rendering – perfect for exploratory design where you create as you go.

content.code:
Row
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Primary
        as Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12 24
        radius 8
        "Save"
    Primary
        "Submit"
    Primary
        background #22c171
        "Confirm"

content.text: Primary as Button defines Primary as a Button type AND renders it. Subsequent uses of Primary inherit the styling. The third button overrides only the background to green.
For containers (divs), the `as Box` part is optional – any unknown name automatically becomes a Box:

content.code:
Card
    background #27272A
    padding 20
    radius 12
    Title
        font-size 18
        weight bold
        "Welcome"
    Card
        // Reuses the Card definition

content.text: Here Card bg #27272A defines AND renders a Card. The nested Card reuses that definition with all its styling.

Interfaces are hierarchical: a card contains a title and text, a header contains a logo and navigation. In HTML, this means nested tags – opening tags, closing tags, and the actual content somewhere in between. In Mirror, indentation expresses this hierarchy directly. Children are indented under their parent. No closing tags, no tag soup – just clear structure.

content.code:
Footer
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    gap 8
    Save-Area
        horizontal
        gap 8
        Button
            background #2271c1
            padding 8 16
            radius 6
            "Save"
        Button
            "Cancel"
    Navigation-Area
        horizontal
        gap 8
        Button
            background #333
            padding 8 16
            radius 6
            "Back"
        Button
            "Next"

content.text: The hor (horizontal) property arranges children side by side. The between property pushes them apart, putting space in the middle. Notice the indentation: Save-Area and Navigation-Area are indented under Footer, so they're children. The buttons are indented further, so they belong to their respective areas.

content.task: Add a third button "Delete" to the Save-Area. Notice how subsequent buttons inherit from the first one!
content.code:
Footer pad 16, hor, between, bg #1a1a1a, rad 8, g 8
  Save-Area hor, g 8
    Button bg #2271c1, pad 8 16, rad 6, "Save"
    Button "Cancel"
  Navigation-Area hor, g 8
    Button bg #333, "Back"
    Button "Next"

content.text: You can also define each component separately at the top. This gives you a clear separation between structure (what things look like) and content (what goes where). The definitions at the top are your design system; the usage below is your layout.

content.code:
Footer:
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    gap 8
Save-Area:
    horizontal
    gap 8
Navigation-Area:
    horizontal
    gap 8

Footer
    Save-Area
        Button
            background #2271c1
            padding 8 16
            radius 6
            "Save"
    Navigation-Area
        Button
            background #333
            padding 8 16
            radius 6
            "Next"

content.text: Once defined, components combine freely. The Footer doesn't care what's inside it – Save-Area, Navigation-Area, or something completely different. This flexibility lets you reuse components in ways you didn't plan for.

content.code:
Footer:
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    gap 8
Save-Area:
    horizontal
    gap 8
Navigation-Area:
    horizontal
    gap 8
Button:
    background #2271c1
    padding 8 16
    radius 6

Footer
    Save-Area
        Button
            "Save"
        Button
            "Cancel"
    Navigation-Area
        Button
            "Back"
        Button
            "Next"

content.text: Components don't enforce their children. A Footer with its between layout works just as well with two buttons directly as it does with nested areas. This loose coupling is intentional – it keeps your options open.

content.code:
Footer:
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    gap 8
Button:
    background #2271c1
    padding 8 16
    radius 6

Footer
    Button
        "Save"
    Button
        "Cancel"

content.text: Even with a definition in place, you can override any property when you use a component. Maybe most buttons are blue, but one needs to stand out. Add properties at usage, and they take precedence.

content.code:
Button
    background #5ba8f5
    padding 8 16
    radius 6
    "Next"

content.text: This Button gets a different background and label, but keeps everything else from the definition. Think of it as: "Use the Button template, but make these specific changes." The definition provides defaults; usage provides overrides.

Sometimes overriding at usage isn't enough. You want a whole category of buttons – Primary, Secondary, Danger – that behave consistently. Inheritance lets you create named variants that share a common base while adding their own character.

content.code:
// Base button
Button:
    padding 8 16
    radius 8

// Variants
Primary-Button:
    Button bg #2271c1
Secondary-Button:
    Button bg transparent
    border 1 #2271c1
Danger-Button:
    Button bg #c14022

Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Primary-Button
        "Save"
    Secondary-Button
        "Cancel"
    Danger-Button
        "Delete"

content.text: The as keyword creates a true parent-child relationship. Primary-Button as Button: means: "Take everything Button has, then add or change these properties." The base defines the shared DNA; variants add their unique traits.

content.code:
Button:
    padding 8 16
    radius 8
Primary-Button as Button:
    bg #2271c1
Secondary-Button as Button:
    bg transparent
    border 1 #2271c1
Danger-Button as Button:
    bg #c14022

Actions
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Primary-Button
        "Save"
    Secondary-Button
        "Cancel"
    Danger-Button
        "Delete"

content.task: Create a GhostBtn variant with transparent background and a blue border.
content.code:
Btn: pad 8 16, rad 8

PrimaryBtn as Btn: bg #2271c1
DangerBtn as Btn: bg #c14022

// Add GhostBtn here (hint: GhostBtn as Btn: bg transparent, bor 1 #2271c1)

Row hor, g 8
  PrimaryBtn "Save"
  DangerBtn "Delete"

content.text: A button that doesn't respond to interaction feels dead. States bring components to life. Define what changes on hover, what happens when focused, how disabled looks different. System states like hover and focus activate automatically – you just describe the visual difference.

content.code:
Button:
    padding 12 24
    background #2271c1
    radius 8
    hover
        background #3388e8

Button
    "Hover me"

content.text: See States section for details on defining states, system states, and hover shorthand.

content.task: Add a state hover with a lighter blue (#5ba8f5).

content.text: Here's where things get powerful: states and inheritance work together. Define a base component with all possible child elements and all relevant states. Variants inherit everything – structure, states, behavior – and only override what makes them different.
The Base Component

This base Button has it all: left icon, label, right icon. Both icons start hidden. It also has hover and disabled states ready to go. Any variant or instance can reveal icons, change text, or activate states – without redefining the structure.

content.code:
Button:
    horizontal
    gap 8
    padding 12 24
    radius 8
    background #2271c1
    color white
    IconLeft:
        Icon "check"
        hidden
    Label:
        "Button"
    IconRight:
        Icon "chevron-right"
        hidden

    hover
        background #1a5a9e
    disabled
        background #94A3B8
        opacity 0.6

Row
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Button
    Button
        IconLeft
            visible
        Label
            "Confirm"
    Button
        disabled
        Label
            "Disabled"

content.text: Variants
Each variant inherits all elements and states from the base. It only specifies what's different.

content.code:
Button:
    horizontal
    gap 8
    padding 12 24
    radius 8
    background #2271c1
    color white
    IconLeft:
        Icon "check"
        hidden
    Label:
        "Button"

Primary-Button as Button:
    IconLeft
        visible

Secondary-Button as Button:
    bg #E2E8F0
    color #1E293B

Ghost-Button as Button:
    bg transparent
    color #2271c1
    border 1 #2271c1

Row
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Primary-Button
        Label
            "Next"
    Secondary-Button
        Label
            "Cancel"
    Ghost-Button
        Label
            "Info"

content.text: Usage
Variants can be used directly, or further customized at instantiation. The disabled state is inherited from the base and available on all variants.

content.code:
Page:
    vertical
    gap 16
    padding 32
    Primary-Button
    Secondary-Button
        Label
            "Cancel"
    Ghost-Button
        Label
            "Learn more"
        IconRight
            visible
    Icon-Button
    Primary-Button
        disabled

content.text: Your Card component always has the same children. But what if you want different actions sometimes? Two buttons, three buttons, or an icon instead? Slots let you define where content goes without prescribing what that content is.

The solution is slots. Inside your component definition, add child components with colons. These become named placeholders – areas where content can go. The slot defines the styling; the usage fills in the content.

content.code:
Card:
    vertical
    padding 24
    gap 16
    background #1A1A23
    radius 12
    Header:
        horizontal
        between
        ver-cen
    Title:
        font-size 20
        weight 600
    Body:
        color #9CA3AF
    Actions:
        horizontal
        gap 8
        hor-r

Card
    Header
        Badge
            background #10B981
            padding 4 8
            radius 4
            font-size 12
            "New"
        Icon
            "more-vertical"
    Title
        "Project Alpha"
    Body
        "A new project with great potential."
    Actions
        Button
            background #333
            padding 8 16
            radius 6
            "Cancel"
        Button
            background #2271c1
            "Save"

content.text: Here's a convenience: you can access nested elements by name directly, regardless of how deep they are. Logo is inside Left which is inside Header – but you just write Logo and Mirror finds it.

content.code:
Header:
    horizontal
    between
    padding 12
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    Left:
        horizontal
        gap 16
        ver-cen
        Logo:
            width 100
            height 32
        Nav:
            horizontal
            gap 8
    Right:
        Avatar:
            width 36
            height 36
            radius 18

// Direct access to nested elements
Header
    Logo
        background #EF4444
    Avatar
        background #2271c1

content.text: This flat access means you can rearrange a component's internal structure without breaking the code that uses it. Move Logo from Left to Right? The usage code stays the same. It's about intent: you want to style Logo, not navigate to it.

content.task: Create a Panel with slots for Header, Body, and Footer.
content.code:
Panel: bg #1A1A23, pad 16, rad 8, g 12
  Header: weight 600
  Body:
  Footer: hor, hor-r

Panel
  Header "My Panel"
  Body "Content here..."
  Footer
    Button bg #2271c1, pad 8 16, rad 6, "OK"

sections.title: Syntax Shortcuts
sections.lead: You understand components and inheritance. Now let's learn shortcuts that make your code even more concise.

content.text: Writing width 300 height 200 every time gets tedious. Mirror lets you skip the keywords: just put numbers after the component name. One number is width, two numbers are width and height.

content.code:
Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Box
        80
        50
        background #2271c1
        radius 8
        center
        Text
            font-size 11
            "80×50"
    Card
        60
        60
        background #333
        radius 8
        center
        Text
            font-size 11
            "60"

Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Box
        80
        50
        background #2271c1
        radius 8
        center
        Text
            font-size 11
            "80×50"
    Card
        60
        background #333
        radius 8
        center
        Text
            font-size 11
            "60"

content.text: One number sets w only. Two numbers set w and height. You can mix shorthand with explicit properties.

Why this shorthand? Width and h are the most common sizing properties. Making them implicit reduces visual noise while keeping code readable. The pattern mirrors how designers think: "a 300 by 400 box".

content.task: Simplify this code using dimension shorthand – remove width and height keywords.
content.code:
Card 280, 180, ver, bg #3281d1, rad 12, pad 16, g 8
  Title weight 600, "Project Alpha"
  Text "A description here"

content.text: For Image, the string is the source URL, followed by optional dimensions:

content.code:
Gallery
    horizontal
    gap 16
    Image
        200
        150
        radius 8
        "https://picsum.photos/id/10/200/150"
    Image
        120
        80
        radius 8
        "https://picsum.photos/id/20/120/80"
    Image
        48
        48
        radius 24
        "https://picsum.photos/id/64/48/48"

content.text: Use opacity for transparency (0–1, where 0 is fully transparent and 1 is fully opaque):

content.code:
Container
    horizontal
    gap 16
    Overlay
        80
        80
        background #000
        opacity 0.5
        radius 8
    Overlay
        80
        80
        background #2271c1
        opacity 0.75
        radius 8
    Overlay
        80
        80
        background #fff
        opacity 0.25
        radius 8

sections.title: Tokens
sections.lead: Ein professionelles Design System basiert auf einem zweistufigen Token-System: Eine Basis-Palette mit reinen Farbwerten und semantische Tokens, die auf diese Palette verweisen.

content.text: Das Token-System in Mirror folgt dem gleichen Prinzip wie professionelle Design-Systeme (Tailwind, Radix, Figma Tokens): Eine Basis-Palette definiert reine Werte, semantische Tokens geben diesen Werten Bedeutung.

Warum zwei Stufen? Stell dir vor, du willst die Primärfarbe ändern. Mit einem einstufigen System müsstest du jeden Button, jeden Link, jedes Highlight einzeln anpassen. Mit dem Zwei-Stufen-System änderst du nur die Referenz – und alles passt sich an.

content.code:
// Stufe 1: Basis-Palette (reine Werte)
$blue-500: #3B82F6
$blue-600: #2563EB
$grey-800: #27272A
$grey-300: #D4D4D8

// Stufe 2: Semantische Tokens (referenzieren Palette)
$primary.bg: $blue-500
$primary.hover.bg: $blue-600
$default.bg: $grey-800
$default.col: $grey-300

// Verwendung
Button
    background $primary.bg
    color #fff
    padding 8 16
    radius 6
    hover
        background $primary.hover.bg

content.text: Jetzt kannst du die Primärfarbe global ändern, indem du nur $primary.bg auf einen anderen Palette-Wert zeigen lässt – z.B. $primary.bg: $green-500.
Die Basis-Palette enthält reine Farbwerte ohne semantische Bedeutung. Das Namensschema folgt dem Muster $farbe-stufe, wobei niedrigere Zahlen heller und höhere Zahlen dunkler sind.

content.code:
// Graustufen (von hell nach dunkel)
$grey-50: #FAFAFA
$grey-100: #F4F4F5
$grey-200: #E4E4E7
$grey-300: #D4D4D8
$grey-400: #A1A1AA
$grey-500: #71717A
$grey-600: #52525B
$grey-700: #3F3F46
$grey-800: #27272A
$grey-900: #18181B
$grey-950: #09090B

// Akzentfarben
$blue-500: #3B82F6
$blue-600: #2563EB
$green-500: #22C55E
$yellow-500: #F59E0B
$red-500: #EF4444

content.text: Diese Palette ist deine Farbgrundlage. Sie sollte alle Farben enthalten, die du in deinem Projekt brauchst. Die Palette selbst hat keine Meinung darüber, was 'primär' oder 'Hintergrund' ist – das kommt erst in der nächsten Stufe.

Semantische Tokens geben den Palette-Werten Bedeutung. Das Besondere in Mirror: Der Property-Name ist Teil des Token-Namens. Das Format ist $name.property – zum Beispiel $primary.bg für den Primär-Hintergrund.
Die Property im Namen zeigt sofort, wofür der Token gedacht ist:

content.code:
// Hintergrund-Hierarchie
$app.bg: $grey-950
$default.bg: $grey-900
$elevated.bg: $grey-800
$surface.bg: $grey-700

// Textfarben
$default.col: $grey-300
$muted.col: $grey-500
$heading.col: $grey-50

// Primary
$primary.bg: $blue-500
$primary.col: $blue-500
$primary.hover.bg: $blue-600
$on-primary.col: #FFFFFF

// Status
$success.bg: $green-500
$danger.bg: $red-500
$warning.bg: $yellow-500

content.text: Beachte die Namenskonvention: $elevated.bg ist 'eine Stufe erhöht' gegenüber $default.bg. Diese Hierarchie hilft dir, konsistente Tiefenwirkung zu erzeugen.
Neben Farben definierst du auch Abstände, Radien und Schriftgrößen als Tokens. Das Namensschema verwendet Größenstufen: xs, sm, md, lg, xl.

content.code:
// Padding
$xs.pad: 2
$sm.pad: 4
$md.pad: 6
$lg.pad: 8
$xl.pad: 12

// Gap
$xs.gap: 2
$sm.gap: 4
$md.gap: 6
$lg.gap: 8

// Radius
$sm.rad: 3
$md.rad: 4
$lg.rad: 6

// Typography (Compound Property)
$xs.font.size: 11
$sm.font.size: 12
$default.font.size: 13
$lg.font.size: 14
$heading.font.size: 15

content.text: Compound Properties wie $xs.font.size haben einen zusätzlichen Teil im Namen (.font), der die Kategorie angibt. Das ist nützlich für Properties, die mehrere Aspekte haben (font-size, font-weight, font-family).
Mit dem zweistufigen System sehen deine Komponenten-Definitionen sauber und wartbar aus. Jeder Wert hat einen semantischen Namen, der seine Funktion erklärt.

content.code:
// Tokens
$grey-800: #27272A
$grey-300: #D4D4D8
$blue-500: #3B82F6
$blue-600: #2563EB

$elevated.bg: $grey-800
$default.col: $grey-300
$primary.bg: $blue-500
$primary.hover.bg: $blue-600
$sm.pad: 4
$lg.pad: 8
$md.rad: 4
$default.font.size: 13

// Komponente
Button:
        horizontal
        center
        background $primary.bg
        color #fff
        padding $sm.pad $lg.pad
        radius $md.rad
        size $default.font.size
        cursor pointer
        hover
                background $primary.hover.bg

Button
    "Click me"

content.text: Wenn du jetzt die Primärfarbe ändern willst, änderst du nur $primary.bg: $green-500 – und alle Buttons, Links und anderen primären Elemente passen sich automatisch an.
Manchmal brauchst du Tokens, die nur für eine bestimmte Komponente gelten. Diese können auf semantische Tokens verweisen – eine dritte Ebene der Indirektion.

content.code:
// Palette
$grey-800: #27272A
$grey-700: #3F3F46
$blue-500: #3B82F6
$blue-600: #2563EB

// Semantic
$primary.bg: $blue-500
$primary.hover.bg: $blue-600

// Component-specific
$button.bg: $primary.bg
$button.hover.bg: $primary.hover.bg
$input.bg: $grey-800
$input.focus.bg: $grey-700

// Verwendung
Button
    background $button.bg
    padding 8 16
    radius 6
    hover
        background $button.hover.bg

Input
    background $input.bg
    padding 8 12
    radius 4
    focus
        background $input.focus.bg

content.text: So kannst du später entscheiden: Sollen alle Buttons der Primärfarbe folgen ($button.bg: $primary.bg) oder eine eigene Farbe haben ($button.bg: $grey-700)? Die Änderung ist trivial.
Hier sind die wichtigsten Regeln für ein wartbares Token-System:

items: Palette zuerst: Definiere alle Basis-Farben, bevor du semantische Tokens erstellst
Semantik referenziert: Semantische Tokens sollten immer auf Palette-Werte verweisen, nie direkte Hex-Codes enthalten
Property im Namen: Verwende .bg, .col, .pad etc. um den Verwendungszweck klar zu machen
Konsistente Stufen: Nutze xs, sm, md, lg, xl für Größenabstufungen
Keine Magic Numbers: Jeder Wert sollte einen Token haben – keine hartcodierten Zahlen in Komponenten

content.code:
// ❌ Schlecht: Magic Numbers und direkte Farbwerte
Button
    background #3B82F6
    padding 8 16
    radius 6

// ✓ Gut: Alles über Tokens
$blue-500: #3B82F6
$primary.bg: $blue-500
$sm.pad: 4
$lg.pad: 8
$md.rad: 4

Button
    background $primary.bg
    padding $sm.pad $lg.pad
    radius $md.rad

content.text: Mit diesem System kannst du das gesamte Erscheinungsbild deiner App ändern, indem du nur die Token-Definitionen anpasst – keine einzige Komponente muss bearbeitet werden.
Neben Tokens kannst du auch Properties von anderen Komponenten referenzieren. Das ist nützlich, wenn du sicherstellen willst, dass zwei Komponenten immer den gleichen Wert haben.

content.code:
Card:
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 24
    radius 12

// Button übernimmt den Radius von Card
Button
    background #3B82F6
    padding 12 24
    radius Card.rad
    "Gleiche Ecken wie Card"

content.text: Wann was verwenden? Tokens für allgemeine Design-Werte, Component References für Werte, die explizit mit einer anderen Komponente synchron bleiben sollen.

sections.title: Typography
sections.lead: Any component with a string becomes text. Properties control how it looks.

items: text-align center – Text alignment (left, center, right)

content.text: No more guessing which property controls what. In Mirror, color always means text color. background always means background color. This consistency eliminates a whole category of confusion that plagues other systems.

content.code:
Box
    gap 8
    Button
        background #2271c1
        color #FFF
        "Blue background, white text"
    Title
        color #2271c1
        "Blue text"

content.task: Create a card with a large bold title and a smaller muted description.
content.code:
Card bg #3281d1, pad 16, rad 8, g 4
  Title text-size 18, weight 600, "Card Title"
  Description text-size 13, "This is a description"

content.text: Format parts of text inline with *text*:style. The style can be bold, italic, underline, or a custom token.

content.code:

    Box

    Box
        "Click *here*:underline to continue"

content.text: Custom Style Tokens
Define a token with multiple style properties and apply it to inline spans.

content.code:
$warning: col #EF4444
weight 700
$highlight: col #22C55E
italic

Box
    gap 8
    Box
        "There is a *critical error*:$warning in your code"
    Box
        "This feature is *new*:$highlight in version 2.0"

content.text: Note: *text* without :style is treated as normal text. Use \* to escape a literal asterisk.
icon displays a Lucide icon. Use the icon name from lucide.dev/icons.

Combine icons with text in buttons. Use horizontal and gap to arrange them.

For icon-only buttons, use cen to center the icon and set equal w and height.

content.task: Create a button with a "plus" icon and the text "Add item".
content.code: Button bg #2271c1, pad 8 16, rad 6, "Add"

items: size N – Font size in pixels
line N – Line h (multiplier or px)

sections.title: Text
sections.lead: For longer text content, Mirror provides a special syntax with formatting tokens for headings, paragraphs, and inline styles.

content.text: Use single quotes for text that spans multiple lines:

content.code:
text
    '$h2 Welcome

    $p This paragraph spans
    multiple lines.

    $p Another paragraph.'

content.text: Block tokens start a line and style all following text:

content.code:
text
    '$h2 Getting Started

    $lead Mirror is a DSL for UI.

    $p Key features:
    $li Simple syntax
    $li Live preview'

content.text: Inline tokens use brackets to format specific phrases:

content.code: text '$p This has bold and _italic_ text. $p Visit [our site](https://example.com).'

sections.title: Layout
sections.lead: You know how to style text. Now let's arrange elements in space – rows, columns, grids, and alignment.

content.text: Layout is about direction. By default, children stack top to bottom – like a document, like natural reading flow. Add horizontal (or just hor) to arrange them side by side instead.

content.code:
Menu
    Item
        "Dashboard"
    Item
        "Projects"
    Item
        "Settings"

Tabs
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Tab
        "Overview"
    Tab
        "Details"
    Tab
        "History"

content.text: Use vertical to explicitly set vertical layout, useful when overriding an inherited horizontal layout.
Elements need breathing room. The gap property sets consistent spacing between all children at once. No need to add margins to individual elements – one gap value handles everything.

content.code:
Actions
    horizontal
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        "Save"
    Button
        "Cancel"
    Button
        "Delete"

content.task: Create a vertical menu with 16px gap between items.
content.code:
Menu ver
  Item bg #27272A, pad 12 16, rad 6, "Dashboard"
  Item "Projects"
  Item "Settings"

content.text: Direction determines the main axis. Alignment controls both axes independently. Want items centered horizontally but stuck to the top? horizontal-center plus vertical-top. The naming is explicit: which axis, which position.

items: horizontal-left / horizontal-center / horizontal-right – Horizontal: left, center, right
vertical-top / vertical-center / vertical-bottom – Vertical: top, center, bottom

content.code:
Header
    horizontal
    ver-cen
    gap 12
    padding 16
    background #2271c1
    Logo
        weight 600
        "App"
    Status
        background #22c55e
        8
        8
        radius 4

content.text: between distributes space between children, pushing them apart. Useful for headers with logo and navigation, or footers with actions on both sides.

content.code:
Header
    horizontal
    between
    ver-cen
    padding 16
    gap 16
    background #2271c1
    Logo
        weight 600
        "Mirror"
    Nav
        horizontal
        gap 16
        Link
            "Docs"
        Link
            "GitHub"

content.text: Use between for space-between distribution along the main axis.

Equal Columns

content.text: When content exceeds its container, you have two choices: clip it or scroll it. Scrollable containers are everywhere – chat feeds, image carousels, settings panels, data tables. Mirror makes them simple.
Vertical Scroll
Use scroll for vertically scrollable content like chat messages or long lists:

content.code:
ChatFeed
    height 150
    scroll
    vertical
    gap 8
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 8
    radius 8
    Message
        background #333
        padding 12
        radius 8

    Message

    Message

    Message

    Message
        background #333

content.text: Most layouts arrange children sequentially. But sometimes you need to layer them – an image with text overlay, an avatar with a status badge, a card with a loading spinner on top. The stacked property makes children occupy the same space, stacking like layers in a design tool.

ImageCard
    stacked
    300
    200
    radius 12
    clip
    Image
        fit cover
        "https://picsum.photos/id/15/300/200"
    Overlay
        vertical
        hor-l
        ver-b
        padding 16
        gap 4
        Title
            weight 600
            "Mountain View"
        Location

            font-size 13
            "Swiss Alps"

content.text: All children occupy the same space. Use alignment (horizontal-left, vertical-bottom) and padding to position the overlay content.
Badge Overlay

Avatar

    80

    Image
        radius 40
        "https://i.pravatar.cc/80"

        background #10B981
        20
        20
        radius 10
        ver-b
        hor-r

sections.title: Forms
sections.lead: With components, tokens, typography, and layout you can build any static UI. But users need to input data. That's where form elements come in.

content.text: Components you've built so far are custom – you control every pixel. But some things need to be HTML natives: text inputs that handle keyboard focus, images that load from URLs, links that navigate. Mirror provides these as primitives: Input, Textarea, Button, Image, and Link.
When you need to reference an input – to read its value, validate it, or react to changes – give it a name. Define it using inheritance syntax: Name as Input: properties.

content.code:
Email as Input:
    "Enter email"
    type email
    padding 12
    background #27272A
    radius 8
Password as Input:
    "Password"
    type password
    padding 12
    background #27272A
    radius 8

LoginForm
    vertical
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 24
    radius 12
    gap 16
    width 300
    Email
    Password
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12 24
        radius 6
        "Login"

content.text: Now you can reference these by name: Email.value, Password.value.

Sometimes you want to define a component AND render it in one step – useful when prototyping or when you only need a component in one place but still want to give it a name. The `as` keyword does exactly this.

content.code:
LoginForm
    vertical
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 24
    radius 12
    gap 16
    width 300
    Email
        as Input
        padding 12
        background #27272A
        radius 8
        "Enter email"
    Password
        as Input
        padding 12
        background #27272A
        radius 8
        type password
        "Password"
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12 24
        radius 6
        "Login"

content.text: Email as Input defines Email as an Input type AND renders it. The component is also registered for reuse – you can use Email elsewhere and it inherits all properties.
For Box (div) components, the `as Box` part is optional. Unknown component names automatically become Box types:

content.code:
Card
    background #27272A
    padding 20
    radius 12
    Title
        font-size 18
        weight bold
        "Welcome"
    Subtitle
        font-size 14
        color #999
        "Get started with Mirror"

content.text: Card, Title, and Subtitle are all defined implicitly as Box types and rendered. You can reuse them later: Card "Different content" inherits the styling.
HTML input types matter – email inputs get mobile keyboards optimized for addresses, password inputs hide characters, number inputs show steppers. Set these with the type property.

content.code:
Username:
    Input "Username"
    type text
Email:
    Input "Email"
    type email
Password:
    Input "Password"
    type password
Age:
    Input "Age"
    type number

Form
    gap 12
    width 280
    Username
    Email
    Password
    Age

content.text: Single-line inputs don't suit every use case. Comments, descriptions, messages – these need room to breathe. Textarea gives you multi-line text input with configurable height.

content.code:
Bio:
    Textarea "Tell us about yourself"
    rows 4
    padding 12
    background #27272A
    radius 8

ProfileForm
    vertical
    gap 12
    width 300
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 24
    radius 12
    Bio

content.text: Primitives aren't locked to browser defaults. You can style them like any other component – padding, borders, backgrounds, focus states. The form becomes part of your design, not a jarring exception.

content.code:
StyledInput:
    Input pad 12 16
    background #0a0a0f
    border 2 #3B82F6
    radius 8

Form
    gap 12
    StyledInput
        "Highlighted input"

content.text: Input and Textarea support Value and Placeholder as styleable children. This gives you fine-grained control over text styling within form fields.

content.code:
StyledInput:
    Input
    width 250
    height 36
    background #27272A
    radius 6
    Placeholder "Enter email..." col #71717A
    Value col #E4E4E7

LoginForm
    gap 12
    StyledInput

content.text: State-Dependent Styling
Combine with state child overrides to change Value or Placeholder styling based on component state:

content.code:
ValidatedInput:
    Input
    width 250
    background #27272A
    radius 6
    Placeholder "Name" col #71717A
    Value col #71717A
    state filled
        Value col #E4E4E7
    state invalid
        Value col #EF4444

Form
    ValidatedInput

content.text: Images are fundamental to visual interfaces. Profile pictures, product photos, illustrations – they all use the Image primitive. The first string is always the source URL.

content.code:
Image

Numbers after the URL set width and height. Add radius for rounded corners:

Use fit to control how images fill their container:

items: fit cover – Fill container, crop if needed (default for most cases)
fit contain – Fit entire image, may leave space
fit fill – Stretch to fill (may distort)

    gap 16
    Box

            100
            100
            radius 8

            "https://picsum.photos/id/29/200/300"
        Text
            font-size 11

            100
            radius 8

            "https://picsum.photos/id/29/200/300"
        Text
            font-size 11
            color #888
            "contain"

content.text: Named Images
Create reusable image definitions with Image Name::

    Image 48
    48
    radius 24
    fit cover

UserRow
    horizontal
    gap 12
    ver-cen
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 12
    radius 8
    Avatar
        "https://i.pravatar.cc/48?u=1"

content.code:
Nav

    gap 16

        "Home"
    Link
        color #888
        "About"
    Link
        color #888
        "Contact"

content.task: Create a user profile card with avatar, name, and a "View Profile" link.
content.code:
Avatar as Image: 64, 64, rad 32, fit cover

ProfileCard hor, g 16, ver-cen, bg #1a1a1a, pad 16, rad 12
  Avatar "https://i.pravatar.cc/64"
  Info ver, g 4
    Name weight 600, "Alice Chen"
