items: Input named Name: – Create named input
Textarea named Name: – Create named textarea
Image named Name: – Create named image
Link named Name: – Create named link
Button named Name: – Create named button

sections.title: Interactivity
sections.lead: Everything so far has been static – components that look good but don't respond to users. Now we'll bring them to life.

content.text: Let's start with the simplest possible interaction: a button that does something when you click it.

content.code:
Box
    vertical
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12
        radius 8
        onclick toggle Message
        "Click me"
    Message
        hidden
        background #10B981
        padding 12
        radius 8
        "Hello! Click again to hide."

content.text: Click the button. The message toggles between visible and hidden. Let's break down what's happening:

items: toggle Message – the action: "toggle visibility of Message"

content.text: The Message starts hidden. Each click toggles it between visible and hidden. This is the most common pattern: a button that shows or hides content.
Now let's see how a component can toggle its own state:

content.code:
Button:
    padding 12
    radius 8
    cursor pointer
    state off
        background #333
        "Light Mode"
    state on
        background #3B82F6
        "Dark Mode"

Button
    onclick toggle

content.text: Now when you click, the button changes color and text. The toggle action cycles through all defined states in order: off → on → off → on...

content.task: Modify the button to have three states: "Low", "Medium", "High" with colors #333, #F59E0B, #EF4444
content.code:
Button: pad 12, rad 8, cursor pointer
  state low
    bg #333, "Low"
  state medium
    bg #F59E0B, "Medium"
  state high
    bg #EF4444, "High"

Button onclick toggle

content.text: Why states instead of variables? You could manage button appearance with variables and conditionals, but states keep all visual variations in one place. You see at a glance every possible appearance of your button – no jumping between files or hunting for where colors are defined.
So far, our button only affects itself. But often you want one element to control another – a button that shows a tooltip, opens a menu, or reveals hidden content.
Show and Hide
The simplest way to control another element is show and hide:

content.code:
Box
    vertical
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12
        radius 8
        onclick show Message
        "Show Message"
    Button
        background #333
        padding 12
        radius 8
        onclick hide Message
        "Hide Message"

    Message:
        hidden
        background #10B981
        padding 12
        radius 8
        color #fff
        "Hello! I was hidden."

content.text: Notice the hidden property on Message – it starts invisible. The first button shows it, the second hides it.
For toggling visibility with a single button, use toggle with a target:

content.code:
Box
    vertical
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12
        radius 8
        onclick toggle Panel
        "Toggle Panel"

    Panel:
        hidden
        background #1E1E2E
        padding 16
        radius 8
        border 1 #333
        "This panel toggles on and off."

content.text: Multiple Actions
What if you want one click to do multiple things? Separate actions with a comma:

content.code:
$count: 0

Box
    vertical
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 12
        radius 8
        "Do Everything"
        onclick show Message
        assign $count to $count + 1

    Message:
        hidden
        background #10B981
        padding 12
        radius 8
        "Shown! Count: " + $count

content.text: Each click shows the message and increments the counter. Actions execute in order, left to right.

items: toggle (no target) – toggle self between states

content.text: We've been using states, but let's understand them properly. A state is a named collection of property overrides. When a state becomes active, its properties replace the defaults.

content.code:
Card:
    padding 16
    background #1E1E2E
    radius 8
    border 1 #333
    cursor pointer
    state default
        "Click to select"
    state selected
        border 2 #3B82F6
        background #1E2A4A
        shadow lg
        "Selected!"

Card
    onclick toggle

content.text: The base Card has bor 1 #333. When the "selected" state activates, it overrides the border to bor 2 #3B82F6 and adds a shadow. Properties not mentioned in the state (like padding and radius) stay the same.
System States
Some states are automatic – the browser activates them based on user interaction:

content.code:
Button:
    padding 12
    radius 8
    background #333
    cursor pointer
    hover
        background #444
    active
        background #222
    "Hover and click me"

Button

content.text: You don't need onclick or any action – the browser handles hover and active states automatically.

items: hover – mouse is over the element

content.text: Hover Shorthand
For simple hover effects, there's a shorthand that keeps everything on one line:

content.code:
Button:
    padding 12
    radius 8
    background #333
    hover
        background #444
        color #fff
    "Hover me"

Button

Button:
    padding 12
    radius 8
    background #333
    hover-bg #444
    hover-col #fff
    "Hover me"

Button

content.text: Available hover shorthands: hover-background, hover-color, hover-border-color, hover-opacity, hover-scale
toggle cycles through states, but sometimes you want to set a specific state. That's what change is for:

content.code:
Status:
    padding 12
    radius 8
    state pending
        background #F59E0B
        "Pending"
    state approved
        background #10B981
        "Approved"
    state rejected
        background #EF4444
        "Rejected"

Box
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Button
        background #333
        padding 8
        radius 4
        onclick change Status to pending
        "Pending"
    Button
        background #333
        padding 8
        radius 4
        onclick change Status to approved
        "Approve"
    Button
        background #333
        padding 8
        radius 4
        onclick change Status to rejected
        "Reject"

Status

content.text: Each button sets the Status to a specific state. Use change when you have more than two states or when you need explicit control over which state to activate.
Understanding self
When you have multiple instances of a component (like three tabs), how do you make each one change only itself? That's where self comes in.
The problem: If you wrote change Tab to active, ALL Tab instances would change. But you want only the clicked tab to become active.
The solution: self refers to the exact instance that received the event – the specific element you just clicked, hovered, or interacted with.

content.code:
Tab:
    padding 12 16
    radius 8
    cursor pointer
    state inactive
        background transparent
        color #666
    state active
        background #2271c1
        color #fff

Box
    horizontal
    gap 4
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 4
    radius 8
    Tab
        onclick change self to active
        "Tab 1"
    Tab
        onclick change self to active
        "Tab 2"
    Tab
        onclick change self to active
        "Tab 3"

content.text: Click any tab – only that specific tab becomes active. The key insight: self always means "me, the element you just interacted with."
You'll use self constantly with repeated components: tabs, list items, cards, buttons in a group. Whenever each instance needs to respond independently, self is the answer.
But wait – clicking one tab doesn't deactivate the others. We need something more...

subsections.title: Tabs: When Only One Can Be Active

content.text: The tabs above have a problem: you can activate multiple tabs at once. In a real tab bar, selecting one should deselect the others. This is called mutual exclusion.
Mirror provides two actions for this pattern:

items: activate self – set your own state to "active"

content.code:
Tab:
    padding 12 16
    cursor pointer
    state inactive
        color #666
        border b 2 transparent
    state active
        color #fff
        border b 2 #3B82F6

TabBar:
    horizontal
    gap 0
    border b 1 #333
    - Tab onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "Overview"
    - Tab onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "Settings"
    - Tab onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "History"

TabBar

content.text: Now only one tab can be active at a time. Click any tab – it activates, and its siblings deactivate.
This pattern works for any mutually exclusive selection: tab bars, toggle groups, radio-like buttons:

content.code:
Option:
    padding 8 16
    radius 4
    cursor pointer
    state inactive
        background transparent
        color #888
    state active
        background #3B82F6
        color #fff

ToggleGroup:
    horizontal
    gap 2
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 4
    radius 8
    - Option onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "Day"
    - Option onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "Week"
    - Option onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "Month"
    - Option onclick activate self
    deactivate-siblings
    "Year"

ToggleGroup

subsections.title: Highlight vs Select: Menus and Lists

content.text: For menus and dropdowns, you need two different concepts:

items: Highlight – temporary visual feedback (hover, keyboard navigation)

content.text: Think about a dropdown menu: as you move your mouse, items highlight. But they're not selected until you click.

content.code:
Item:
    padding 8 12
    cursor pointer
    state default
        background transparent
    state highlighted
        background #333
    state selected
        background #3B82F6

Menu:
    vertical
    background #1E1E2E
    radius 8
    padding 4
    width 200
    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Dashboard"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Settings"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Profile"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Logout"

Menu

content.text: Hover over items – they highlight (gray). Click one – it gets selected (blue). The highlight is temporary; the selection persists.

items: highlight next / prev – highlight next/previous sibling
select highlighted – select whatever is currently highlighted

content.text: Good UIs are keyboard accessible. Mirror makes this easy with onkeydown:

content.code:
Item:
    padding 8 12
    cursor pointer
    state default
        background transparent
    state highlighted
        background #333

Menu:
    vertical
    background #1E1E2E
    radius 8
    padding 4
    width 200
    onkeydown arrow-down highlight next
    onkeydown arrow-up highlight prev
    onkeydown home highlight first
    onkeydown end highlight last

    - Item onhover highlight self
    "First Item"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    "Second Item"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    "Third Item"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    "Fourth Item"

Menu

content.text: Click the menu to focus it, then use arrow keys to navigate. Home jumps to first, End to last.

items: arrow-up, arrow-down, arrow-left, arrow-right – Arrow keys

content.text: Navigation Targets
Actions like highlight and focus can target relative positions within a container:

items: self – this specific instance (the one you clicked/hovered)
next – next sibling in the container
prev – previous sibling in the container
first – first element in the container
last – last element in the container

content.text: Remember: self is essential when you have multiple instances of a component. It ensures only the interacted element responds, not all elements of that type.
When you have many keyboard handlers, the keys block provides a cleaner syntax. The colon after the key name is optional:

content.code:
Menu:
    vertical
    padding 8
    keys
        escape close
        arrow-down highlight next
        arrow-up highlight prev
        enter select, close

Menu
    - Item "Dashboard"
    - Item "Settings"

content.text: This is equivalent to writing each onkeydown separately, but much more readable when you have several keyboard shortcuts.
For common actions, self is the default target and can be omitted:

content.code:
// These are equivalent:
onclick select self
onclick select

onhover highlight self
onhover highlight

onclick-outside close self
onclick-outside close

content.text: This works for: highlight, select, deselect, activate, deactivate, toggle-state, close, show, hide.
When you need a different target, specify it explicitly: onclick close Menu or onclick highlight next.
Let's combine everything into a fully functional dropdown with mouse and keyboard support:

content.code:
Item:
    padding 8 12
    cursor pointer
    state default
        background transparent
    state highlighted
        background #333
    state selected
        background #3B82F6

Trigger:
    padding 8 12
    background #1E1E2E
    radius 8
    border 1 #333
    cursor pointer
    onclick toggle Dropdown
    "Select an option..."

Dropdown:
    vertical
    background #1E1E2E
    radius 8
    shadow lg
    hidden
    width 200
    onclick-outside close self
    onkeydown escape close self
    onkeydown arrow-down highlight next
    onkeydown arrow-up highlight prev
    onkeydown enter select highlighted
    close self

    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Dashboard"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Settings"
    - Item onhover highlight self
    onclick select self
    "Profile"

Trigger
Dropdown

content.text: This dropdown:
Some interactions need timing control. Two common cases:
Debounce: Wait for User to Stop Typing
For search/autocomplete, you don't want to filter on every keystroke – wait until the user pauses:

content.code:
$query: ""

Box
    vertical
    gap 12
    width 300
    Input:
        padding 8 12
        background #1E1E2E
        radius 8
        border 1 #333
        oninput debounce 300 assign $query to $event.value
        "Type something..."

    Text
        color #666
        "Query: " + $query

content.text: debounce 300 waits 300ms after the last keystroke before executing the action. If the user keeps typing, the timer resets.
Delay: Wait Before Acting
Sometimes you need a brief pause before an action. Common use: keeping a dropdown open long enough to click it:

content.code:
Box
    vertical
    gap 8
    width 200
    Input:
        padding 8
        background #1E1E2E
        radius 8
        border 1 #333
        onfocus show Results
        onblur delay 150 hide Results
        "Focus me..."

    Results:
        vertical
        background #1E1E2E
        radius 8
        shadow lg
        hidden
        - Item pad 8
        hover-bg #333
        cursor pointer
        "Result 1"
        - Item pad 8
        hover-bg #333
        cursor pointer
        "Result 2"
        - Item pad 8
        hover-bg #333
        cursor pointer
        "Result 3"

content.text: Without the delay, clicking a result would blur the input and hide the results before the click registers. The 150ms delay gives you time to click.

items: debounce N – wait N ms after last event (resets on each event)
delay N – wait N ms before executing

content.text: Accordions expand and collapse sections. Use toggle-state to flip between two states:

content.code:
Section:
    vertical
    border b 1 #333
    Header:
        horizontal
        between
        padding 12
        cursor pointer
        hover-bg #1a1a1a
        Title:
            weight 500
        Icon:
            color #666
            "chevron-down"
    Content:
        padding 12
        background #1a1a1a

    state collapsed
        Content
            hidden
    state expanded
        Content
            visible

Box
    vertical
    Section
        Header
            onclick toggle-state
            Title
                "Section 1"
        Content
            "Content for section 1. Click header to collapse."
    Section
        collapsed
        Header
            onclick toggle-state
            Title
                "Section 2"
        Content
            "Content for section 2."
    Section
        collapsed
        Header
            onclick toggle-state
            Title
                "Section 3"
        Content
            "Content for section 3."

content.text: toggle-state cycles through the component's defined states – here, between "collapsed" and "expanded".
Rating components need a special behavior: hovering over star 3 should highlight stars 1, 2, and 3. That's the self-and-before target:

content.code:
Star:
    cursor pointer
    state empty
        Icon
            color #333
            icon-size 24
            "star"
    state highlighted
        Icon
            color #F59E0B
            icon-size 24
            "star"
    state selected
        Icon
            color #F59E0B
            icon-size 24
            "star"

Rating:
    horizontal
    gap 4
    - Star onhover highlight self-and-before
    onclick select self-and-before
    - Star onhover highlight self-and-before
    onclick select self-and-before
    - Star onhover highlight self-and-before
    onclick select self-and-before
    - Star onhover highlight self-and-before
    onclick select self-and-before
    - Star onhover highlight self-and-before
    onclick select self-and-before

Rating

content.text: Hover over the third star – all three before it (including itself) highlight. Click to make the selection permanent.
Visibility

items: open Target [position] – Open overlay (positions: below, above, left, right, center)
close [Target] – Close overlay (self if no target)

content.text: State Control

items: change self to stateName – Set element to specific state
change Target to stateName – Set another element's state
toggle-state stateName – Toggle between two states

content.text: Selection & Highlighting

items: highlight Target – Highlight an element (highlighted state)
select Target – Select an element (selected state)
clear-selection – Clear all selections in container

content.text: Data & Forms

items: assign $variable to value – Set a variable's value
filter Container – Filter items in container by input value
reset FormName – Reset form to initial values
focus Target – Set focus to element

content.text: Navigation

items: page PageName – Navigate to a different page

content.text: JavaScript Integration (planned)

items: call functionName – Call an external JavaScript function (NOT YET IMPLEMENTED)

content.code:
// Filter example: search input filters a list
$query: ""

Search
    as Input pad 12
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    "Search..."
    oninput assign $query to $event.value
    filter Results

Results
    vertical
    gap 8
    - Item bg #222
    padding 12
    radius 6
    "Dashboard"
    - Item bg #222
    padding 12
    radius 6
    "Settings"
    - Item bg #222
    padding 12
    radius 6
    "Profile"

content.text: Actions can target elements in various ways:

items: self – The element that triggered the event
first-empty – First empty element (useful for form inputs)
self-and-before – Self and all elements before it
all – All elements of that type

content.code:
Rating
    horizontal
    gap 4
    Star:
        24
        24
        cursor pointer
        onclick select self-and-before
        state default
            Icon
                color #666
                "star"
        state selected
            Icon
                color #F59E0B
                fill
                "star"

    - Star
    - Star
    - Star
    - Star
    - Star

content.text: The self-and-before target is perfect for rating widgets – clicking the third star selects it and all stars before it.
⚠️ Note: The call action is documented for future use but NOT YET IMPLEMENTED in the current parser.
When implemented, call will let you invoke JavaScript functions from Mirror events, bridging prototypes and production code:

sections.title: Named Instances
sections.lead: We've learned how events trigger actions. But how do we know what the user entered in a form? How do we target a specific button among many?

content.text: Named instances can also be targeted for styling. Consider a footer with multiple buttons – which one should turn red when you write Footer Button #F00? Mirror can't know. Name them:

content.code:
Footer:
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    Button
        named Save: bg #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        "Save"
    Button
        named Cancel: bg #333
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        "Cancel"

Footer

content.text: Now you can target specific instances:

content.code:
Footer:
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    Button
        named Save: pad 8 16
        radius 6
        "Save"
    Button
        named Cancel: bg #333
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        "Cancel"

Footer
    Save
        background #10B981  // Target Save specifically

content.text: Mirror finds Save within Footer and changes its color. Named instances can also be hidden:

content.code:
Footer:
    padding 16
    horizontal
    between
    background #1a1a1a
    radius 8
    Button
        named Save: bg #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        "Save"
    Button
        named Cancel: bg #333
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        "Cancel"

Footer
    Cancel
        hidden  // Only Save is visible

content.text: Every component has intrinsic properties you can read and write:

content.code:
Email:
    Input bg #1a1a1a
    padding 12
    radius 8
    border 1 #333

Card
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 16
    radius 8
    gap 12
    Email
        "Type something..."
    Text
        color #888
        font-size 13
        if Email.value then "You typed: " + Email.value else "Input is empty"
    Button
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        if Email.value then bg #2271c1 else bg #333
        if Email.value then "Submit" else "Enter text first"

content.text: Components ARE the state. No separate state management needed. Email.value is the email – simple as that.

Why this approach? Traditional frameworks require separate state variables (useState) that mirror UI values. In Mirror, the component is the source of truth. This eliminates the "state/UI sync problem" that plagues many applications.

content.task: Create a form where clicking Submit shows an alert with the Email value (use if Email.value to check if it's filled).
content.code:
Email as Input: "Enter email"
Submit as Button: bg #2271c1, pad 12, rad 8, "Submit"

Form bg #1a1a1a, pad 24, rad 12, g 16, w 300
  Email
  Submit

sections.title: Overlays
sections.lead: You can now trigger actions and access component properties. A common pattern is showing content that floats above the UI – dialogs, menus, tooltips.

content.text: A modal dialog that blocks interaction with the rest of the page. Define a trigger button and the dialog content:

content.code:
Button
    onclick open SettingsDialog
    "Open Settings"

SettingsDialog
    as Dialog: bg #1A1A23
    padding 24
    radius 12
    width 400
    Title
        font-size 18
        weight 600
        "Settings"
    Text
        "Configure your preferences here."
    Actions
        horizontal
        gap 8
        hor-r
        Button
            background #333
            onclick close
            "Cancel"
        Button
            background #2271c1
            onclick close
            "Save"

content.text:
open SettingsDialog shows the dialog. close hides it.
        Dialogs automatically close when clicking outside or pressing Escape.

content.task: Click the button to open the dialog. Close it with the buttons or click outside.
content.code:
SettingsDialog as Dialog: bg #1A1A23, pad 24, rad 12, w 320
  Title text-size 18, weight 600, "Settings"
  Text col #888, "Configure your preferences here."
  Actions hor, g 8, hor-r
    Button bg #333, onclick close, "Cancel"
    Button bg #2271c1, onclick close, "Save"

Button onclick open SettingsDialog center, "Open Settings"

content.text: Overlays can be positioned relative to their trigger element:

content.code:
Menu
    as Dropdown:
    Trigger
        background #333
        padding 8 12
        radius 6
        "Options ▼"
    Content
        vertical
        gap 2
        background #1a1a1a
        padding 4
        radius 8
        border 1 #333
        Item
            padding 8 12
            radius 4
            hover-bg #333
            "Profile"
        Item
            "Settings"
        Item
            background #EF4444
            "Logout"

Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Menu

items: left – to the left of trigger
right – to the right of trigger

content.text: A menu that opens below its trigger:

content.code:
OptionsMenu
    as Dropdown:
    Trigger:
        padding 8 12
        background #1A1A23
        radius 6
        border 1 #333
        "Select option"
    Content:
        vertical
        gap 2
        background #1A1A23
        radius 8
        padding 4
        - Item pad 8 12
        radius 4
        hover-bg #333
        "Profile"
        - Item "Settings"
        - Item "Logout"

OptionsMenu

content.text: A small hint that appears on hover:

content.code:
HelpTip
    as Tooltip:
    Trigger
        Button
            Icon
                "help-circle"
            "?"
    Content:
        padding 8 12
        background #333
        radius 6
        font-size 12
        "Click to get help"

HelpTip

content.text: Overlays can have entrance and exit animations:

content.code:
FadeDialog
    as Dialog: bg #1a1a1a
    padding 24
    radius 12
    width 250
    show fade 200
    Title
        weight 600
        "Fade Animation"
    Button
        background #333
        onclick close
        "Close"

ScaleDialog
    as Dialog: bg #1a1a1a
    padding 24
    radius 12
    width 250
    show fade scale 200
    Title
        weight 600
        "Scale Animation"
    Button
        background #333
        onclick close
        "Close"

Row
    horizontal
    gap 8
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 8
        onclick open FadeDialog
        "Fade"
    Button
        background #333
        padding 8 16
        radius 8
        onclick open ScaleDialog
        "Scale"

content.task: Create a button that opens a dialog. Add a close button inside the dialog.
content.code:
Button bg #2271c1, pad 12, rad 8, onclick open MyDialog, "Open Dialog"

MyDialog as Dialog: bg #1A1A23, pad 24, rad 12, w 300, ver, g 16
  Title text-size 18, weight 600, "Hello!"
  Text "This is a dialog."
  Button bg #333, onclick close, "Close"

sections.title: Animations
sections.lead: Overlays appear and disappear. Animations smooth the transitions between visibility states.

content.text: Use show and hide blocks to define how an element animates in and out:

content.code:
Card
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 16
    radius 8
    gap 12
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        onclick toggle Panel
        "Toggle Panel"
    Panel
        hidden
        background #333
        padding 16
        radius 8
        show fade slide-up 300
        hide fade 150
        Text
            "This panel fades and slides!"

content.text:
The panel starts hidden (hidden). When it becomes visible, it fades in and slides up (300ms).
        When hiding, it fades out (200ms). Animations can be combined: fade slide-up.

content.code:
Card
    background #1a1a1a
    padding 16
    radius 8
    gap 12
    Button
        background #333
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        onclick toggle Box
        "Toggle Animation"
    Box
        hidden
        horizontal
        gap 8
        show fade slide-up 300
        Tile
            background #2271c1
            60
            60
            radius 8
            center
            "fade"
        Tile
            background #10B981
            60
            60
            radius 8
            center
            "slide"
        Tile
            background #EF4444
            60
            60
            radius 8
            center
            "scale"

content.text: ⚠️ Note: Continuous animations (animate spin, pulse, bounce) are NOT YET IMPLEMENTED in the current parser. The syntax below is documented for future use.

content.code:
Loader
    24
    24
    animate spin 1000
    Icon
        "loader"

content.text: When implemented, the icon will rotate continuously, completing one full rotation every 1000ms. Currently only show/hide animations (fade, slide-up, etc.) work.
An expandable card with animations:

content.code:
Card
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    Header
        horizontal
        between
        ver-cen
        onclick toggle Details
        Title
            weight 600
            "Click to expand"
        Icon
            "chevron-down"
    Details
        hidden
        show fade slide-down 200
        hide fade 150
        Text
            padding t 12
            "Here are the details..."

content.task: Add a show animation to make the content fade in when revealed.
content.code:
Card bg #1A1A23, pad 16, rad 8, ver, g 12
  Button bg #333, onclick toggle Content, "Toggle Content"
  Content hidden
    Text "This content can be toggled"

sections.title: Variables & State
sections.lead: You've built static interfaces – components that always look the same. Now let's make them come alive. A button that counts clicks. A toggle that remembers its state. A display that updates in real-time.

content.text: Variables start with $ and are defined with a colon:

content.code:
$count: 0
$name: "World"
$isActive: true

Card
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    vertical
    gap 8
    Text
        "Count: " + $count
    Text
        "Hello, " + $name
    Text
        if $isActive then "Active" else "Inactive"

content.text: Variables can hold numbers, text, or boolean values (true/false). Use + to combine text with variables.
Use assign in event handlers to change a variable's value:

content.code:
$count: 0

Counter
    horizontal
    gap 16
    ver-cen
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    Button
        background #333
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        onclick assign $count to $count - 1
        "-"
    Text
        font-size 24
        weight 600
        width 60
        text-align center
        $count
    Button
        background #2271c1
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        onclick assign $count to $count + 1
        "+"

content.text: Click the buttons – the count updates instantly. The Text component automatically reflects the new value because it displays $count.
A common pattern is toggling between two states. Use not to flip a boolean:

content.code:
$liked: false

LikeButton
    horizontal
    gap 8
    ver-cen
    padding 12 16
    radius 8
    cursor pointer
    if $liked then bg #DC2626 else bg #333
    onclick assign $liked to not $liked
    Icon
        icon-size 18
        "heart"
    Text
        if $liked then "Liked" else "Like"

content.text: Mirror supports basic math: +, -, *, /

content.code:
$price: 29
$quantity: 2

Cart
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    vertical
    gap 12
    Row
        horizontal
        between
        Text
            "Price:"
        Text
            "$" + $price
    Row
        horizontal
        between
        Text
            "Quantity:"
        Row
            horizontal
            gap 8
            Button
                background #333
                padding 4 12
                radius 4
                onclick assign $quantity to $quantity - 1
                "-"
            Text
                width 30
                text-align center
                $quantity
            Button
                background #333
                padding 4 12
                radius 4
                onclick assign $quantity to $quantity + 1
                "+"
    Divider
        height 1
        background #333
    Row
        horizontal
        between
        Text
            weight 600
            "Total:"
        Text
            weight 600
            color #10B981
            "$" + ($price * $quantity)

content.task: Create a temperature converter. Define $celsius: 20 and display Fahrenheit ($celsius * 9 / 5 + 32).
content.code:
$celsius: 20

Converter bg #1A1A23, pad 16, rad 8, ver, g 12
  Row hor, g 8, ver-cen
    Button bg #333, pad 8, rad 4, onclick assign $celsius to $celsius - 5, "-5"
    Text text-size 24, weight 600, $celsius + "°C"
    Button bg #333, pad 8, rad 4, onclick assign $celsius to $celsius + 5, "+5"
  // Add Fahrenheit display here

content.text: Variables make your UI reactive. Change a value once, and everything that depends on it updates automatically. This is the foundation for dynamic interfaces.

sections.title: Conditional Display
sections.lead: Your UI responds to clicks and updates values. But what if you want to show completely different content based on state? A login button that becomes a logout button. An error message that only appears when needed. A theme that switches between light and dark.

content.text: Change a single property based on a condition with if ... then ... else:

content.code:
$isOnline: true

Status
    horizontal
    gap 8
    ver-cen
    background #1A1A23
    padding 12
    radius 8
    Dot
        8
        8
        radius 4
        if $isOnline then bg #10B981 else bg #666
    Text
        if $isOnline then "Online" else "Offline"
    Button
        background #333
        padding 6 12
        radius 4
        font-size 12
        onclick assign $isOnline to not $isOnline
        "Toggle"

content.text: The dot and text both respond to $isOnline. One condition, multiple effects.
When you want to show different components entirely, use if / else blocks:

content.code:
$loggedIn: false

Header
    horizontal
    between
    ver-cen
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    Logo
        weight 600
        "MyApp"
    if $loggedIn
        Row
            horizontal
            gap 12
            ver-cen
            Avatar
                32
                32
                radius 16
                background #2271c1
            Button
                background #333
                padding 8 16
                radius 6
                onclick assign $loggedIn to false
                "Logout"
    else
        Button
            background #2271c1
            padding 8 16
            radius 6
            onclick assign $loggedIn to true
            "Login"

content.text: The if block shows the avatar and logout button when logged in. The else block shows the login button otherwise. Completely different UI structures, controlled by one variable.
Conditions can compare values: == (equal), != (not equal), >, <, >=, <=

content.code:
$score: 75

Result
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    vertical
    gap 12
    Row
        horizontal
        gap 8
        ver-cen
        Text
            "Score:"
        Button
            background #333
            padding 4 8
            radius 4
            onclick assign $score to $score - 10
            "-10"
        Text
            weight 600
            font-size 20
            $score
        Button
            background #333
            padding 4 8
            radius 4
            onclick assign $score to $score + 10
            "+10"
    Badge
        padding 8 16
        radius 6
        if $score >= 90 then bg #10B981 else if $score >= 70 then bg #F59E0B else bg #EF4444
        if $score >= 90 then "Excellent" else if $score >= 70 then "Good" else "Needs Work"

content.text: Combine conditions with and, or, and not:

content.code:
$hasEmail: true
$hasPassword: false

Form
    background #1A1A23
    padding 16
    radius 8
    vertical
    gap 12
    Row
        horizontal
        gap 8
        Checkbox
            18
            18
            radius 4
            if $hasEmail then bg #2271c1 else bg #333
            onclick assign $hasEmail to not $hasEmail
        Text
            "Email entered"
    Row
        horizontal
        gap 8
        Checkbox
            18
            18
            radius 4
            if $hasPassword then bg #2271c1 else bg #333
            onclick assign $hasPassword to not $hasPassword
        Text
            "Password entered"
    Button
        padding 12
        radius 6
        w-max
        if $hasEmail and $hasPassword then bg #2271c1 else bg #333
        if $hasEmail and $hasPassword then "Submit" else "Fill all fields"

content.task: Create a theme switcher with a $darkMode variable. Change the card background between #1A1A23 (dark) and #F5F5F5 (light).
content.code:
$darkMode: true

Card pad 24, rad 12, ver, g 16
  if $darkMode then bg #1A1A23 else bg #F5F5F5
  Title text-size 20, weight 600
    if $darkMode then col white else col #1A1A23
    "Theme Demo"
  Text
    if $darkMode then col #9CA3AF else col #666
    "Click to switch themes"
  Button pad 12, rad 6
    if $darkMode then bg #2271c1 else bg #1A1A23
    onclick assign $darkMode to not $darkMode
    if $darkMode then "Switch to Light" else "Switch to Dark"

content.text: Conditions turn static layouts into adaptive interfaces. The same code handles multiple states – you just describe what each state looks like.

sections.title: Lists & Data
sections.lead: You can show and hide elements, change their properties. But what about a list of 50 products? 100 messages? You're not going to type each one. Real apps get data – from APIs, databases, user input – and generate UI from it.

content.text: Use each $item in $list to create a component for every item:

content.code:
// === DATA TAB ===
Fruit:
    name: text

- Fruit "Apple"
- Fruit "Banana"
- Fruit "Cherry"
- Fruit "Date"

// === LAYOUT TAB ===
List
    data Fruits
    vertical
    gap 8
    Item
        background #1A1A23
        padding 12
        radius 6
        Text
            $item.name

content.text: Four items in the array, four components rendered. The variable $fruit holds each value in turn.
Real data usually has structure. Access properties with dot notation:

content.code:
// === DATA TAB ===
User:
    name: text
    role: text
    online: boolean

- User "Alice"
"Admin"
true
- User "Bob"
"Editor"
true
- User "Carol"
"Viewer"
false

// === LAYOUT TAB ===
UserList
    data Users
    vertical
    gap 8
    Card
        horizontal
        between
        ver-cen
        background #1A1A23
        padding 12
        radius 8
        Left
            horizontal
            gap 12
            ver-cen
            Status
                width 8
                height 8
                radius 4
                if $item.online then bg #10B981 else bg #666
            Name
                weight 500
                $item.name
        Role
            color #888
            font-size 13
            $item.role

content.text: Iterate within iterations for hierarchical data:

content.code:
// === DATA TAB ===
MenuItem:
    name: text
    category: text

- MenuItem "Soup"
"Starters"
- MenuItem "Salad"
"Starters"
- MenuItem "Bread"
"Starters"
- MenuItem "Pasta"
"Mains"
- MenuItem "Steak"
"Mains"
- MenuItem "Fish"
"Mains"
- MenuItem "Cake"
"Desserts"
- MenuItem "Ice Cream"
"Desserts"

// === LAYOUT TAB ===
Menu
    vertical
    gap 16
    Section
        vertical
        gap 8
        Title
            weight 600
            color #2271c1
            "Category Name"
        Items
            data MenuItems
            vertical
            gap 4
            Item
                background #1A1A23
                padding 8 12
                radius 6
                $item.name

content.text: Capture user input with $event.value:

content.code:
// === DATA TAB ===
MenuItem:
    label: text

- MenuItem "Dashboard"
- MenuItem "Settings"
- MenuItem "Profile"
- MenuItem "Messages"
- MenuItem "Help"

// === LAYOUT TAB ===
$search: ""

Search
    vertical
    gap 12
    width 250
    Input
        background #1A1A23
        padding 12
        radius 8
        border 1 #333
        "Search..."
        oninput assign $search to $event.value
    Results
        data MenuItems
        vertical
        gap 4
        Item
            background #1A1A23
            padding 8 12
            radius 6
            $item.label

content.text: Filter displayed items by combining each with if:

content.code:
// === DATA TAB ===
Task:
    title: text
    done: boolean

- Task "Design mockup" true
- Task "Build prototype" false
- Task "User testing" false
- Task "Write docs" true

// === LAYOUT TAB ===
$showDone: true

TaskList
    vertical
    gap 12
    Row
        horizontal
        gap 8
        ver-cen
        Checkbox
            width 18
            height 18
            radius 4
            cursor pointer
            if $showDone then bg #2271c1 else bg #333
            onclick assign $showDone to not $showDone
        Text
            font-size 13
            color #888
            "Show completed"
    Tasks
        data Tasks
        vertical
        gap 8
        if $showDone or not $item.done
            TaskRow
                horizontal
                gap 12
                ver-cen
                background #1A1A23
                padding 12
                radius 8
                Icon
                    if $item.done then col #10B981 else col #333
                    if $item.done then "check-circle" else "circle"
                Text
                    if $item.done then col #666 else col white
                    $item.title

content.task: Create a contact list with name, email, and a status indicator. Use $contact.active to show green/gray dots.

content.text: Data-driven UIs scale effortlessly. Whether you have 3 items or 3000, the same template handles them all. The UI becomes a view of your data.

sections.title: Data Tab
sections.lead: For complex prototypes with structured data, the Data tab provides a cleaner way to define schemas and instances separately from your layout code.

content.text: A schema defines the structure of your data. Use a colon after the type name, then indent fields with their types:

content.code:
// In the Data tab:
Task:
    title: text
    done: boolean
    priority: number

Category:
    name: text
    color: text

content.text: Each field has a name and a type. Available types are:
After defining schemas, create instances using - prefix. Values are listed in field order:

content.code:
// Schema
Category:
    name: text
    color: text

// Instances (values in field order, comma-separated)
- Category "Arbeit"
"#3B82F6"
- Category "Privat"
"#10B981"
- Category "Dringend"
"#EF4444"

content.text: For schemas with many fields:

content.code:
Task:
    title: text
    done: boolean
    priority: number

// Instances: title, done, priority (comma-separated)
- Task "Einkaufen"
false
1
- Task "Sport machen"
true
2
- Task "Meeting vorbereiten"
false
3

content.text: Use index notation to reference other instances. Category[0] refers to the first Category instance:

content.code:
Category:
    name: text

Task:
    title: text
    done: boolean
    category: Category    // Reference to Category type

// Categories
- Category "Arbeit"
- Category "Privat"

// Tasks referencing categories by index (comma-separated)
- Task "Email schreiben"
false
Category[0]    // References "Arbeit"
- Task "Joggen gehen"
true
Category[1]        // References "Privat"
- Task "Report erstellen"
false
Category[0]   // References "Arbeit"

content.text: The index [0] refers to the first instance of that type, [1] to the second, and so on.
Use the data property to bind a container to your data. The container repeats its children for each record:

content.code:
// In Data tab:
Task:
    title: text
    done: boolean

- Task "Buy groceries"
false
- Task "Call mom"
true

// In Layout tab:
TaskList
    data Tasks
    vertical
    gap 8
    Card
        padding 12
        radius 8

content.text: Note: Field binding syntax (Tasks.title) to display data values directly is planned for a future release.
Here's a full example with multiple related types:

content.code:
// ===== DATA TAB =====

// Schema definitions
Category:
    name: text
    icon: text
    color: text

Task:
    title: text
    done: boolean
    category: Category

// Category instances
- Category "Work"
"briefcase"
"#3B82F6"
- Category "Personal"
"user"
"#10B981"
- Category "Urgent"
"alert-circle"
"#EF4444"

// Task instances
- Task "Prepare presentation"
false
Category[0]
- Task "Buy birthday gift"
false
Category[1]
- Task "Fix critical bug"
false
Category[2]
- Task "Morning workout"
true
Category[1]
- Task "Team meeting"
true
Category[0]

// ===== LAYOUT TAB =====

// The container repeats for each Task record
TaskList
    data Tasks
    vertical
    gap 8
    padding 16
    Card
        horizontal
        between
        ver-cen
        background #1A1A23
        padding 12
        radius 8
        Left
            horizontal
            gap 12
            ver-cen
            Icon
                "file-text"
            Title
                "Task item"
        Status
            Icon
                color #333
                "circle"

content.text: When you've defined schemas in the Data tab, a Generate button appears. Click it to have AI create realistic sample data based on your schema definitions.
The AI understands your field names and types, generating contextually appropriate values:

items: email: text → Generates realistic email addresses
price: number → Generates appropriate price values

content.text: This is especially useful for prototyping: define your data structure once, then generate 10, 20, or 50 realistic entries instantly.
A common UI pattern is Master-Detail: a list (master) where clicking an item shows its details (detail). Mirror supports this through $item assignment:

content.code:
// === DATA TAB ===
Task:
    title: text
    description: text
    done: boolean

- Task "Einkaufen"
"Milch, Brot, Eier holen"
false
- Task "Sport"
"Joggen im Park"
true
- Task "Meeting"
"Team-Sync um 14 Uhr"
false

// === LAYOUT TAB ===
$selectedTask: null

App
    horizontal
    gap 24
    padding 24

  // MASTER: Task-Liste
    Master
        vertical
        gap 8
        width 250
        List
            data Tasks
            - TaskItem pad 12
            background #1A1A23
            radius 8
            cursor pointer
                onclick assign $selectedTask to $item
                hover
                    background #252530
                Text
                    $item.title

  // DETAIL: Ausgewählter Task
    Detail
        w-max
        if $selectedTask
            Card
                padding 24
                background #1A1A23
                radius 12
                Text
                    font-size 24
                    weight 600
                    $selectedTask.title
                Text
                    color #888
                    $selectedTask.description
                Box
                    horizontal
                    gap 8
                    margin t 16
                    Icon
                        if $selectedTask.done then "check-circle" else "circle"
                    Text
                        if $selectedTask.done then "Erledigt" else "Offen"
        else
            Box
                center
                height 200
                Text
                    color #666
                    "Wähle einen Task aus"

content.text: Key points:

items: onclick assign $selectedTask to $item – Store the clicked record
$selectedTask.title – Access fields of the selected record
if $selectedTask – Show detail only when something is selected

content.text: Inside a data-bound loop, $item contains the current record with all its fields. Assigning $item to a variable preserves the entire record for use elsewhere.

sections.title: Interactive Components
sections.lead: You've built data-driven interfaces. Now let's add sophisticated interaction patterns – keyboard navigation, click-outside-to-close, selection states. The kind of behavior that makes a dropdown feel polished.

content.text: Beyond system states like hover, you can define custom states that change through actions:

content.code:
Item:
    padding 12 16
    radius 6
    cursor pointer
    state default
        background transparent
    state highlighted
        background #333
    state selected
        background #2271c1

Menu
    vertical
    gap 2
    background #1A1A23
    padding 8
    radius 8
    width 200
    Item
        onhover highlight self
        "Dashboard"
    Item
        onhover highlight self
        "Settings"
    Item
        onhover highlight self
        "Profile"

content.text: highlight self activates the highlighted state. The state defines what changes visually. Hover over the items to see it in action.
Handle keyboard events with onkeydown plus a key name:

content.code:
Item:
    padding 12 16
    radius 6
    cursor pointer
    state default
        background transparent
    state highlighted
        background #333

Menu
    vertical
    gap 2
    background #1A1A23
    padding 8
    radius 8
    width 200
    onkeydown arrow-down: highlight next
    onkeydown arrow-up: highlight prev
    Item
        "Dashboard"
    Item
        "Settings"
    Item
        "Profile"
    Item
        "Logout"

content.text: Click the menu to focus it, then use arrow keys. highlight next and highlight prev move through siblings automatically.
Close dropdowns when clicking elsewhere with onclick-outside:

content.code:
Trigger:
    background #2271c1
    padding 12 16
    radius 8
    cursor pointer
    onclick toggle Dropdown

Dropdown:
    vertical
    background #1A1A23
    padding 8
    radius 8
    width 180
    hidden
    shadow lg
    onclick-outside close self
    Item:
        padding 10 14
        radius 6
        cursor pointer
        hover-bg #333

Box
    vertical
    gap 8
    Trigger
        "Open Menu"
    Dropdown
        Item
            "Edit"
        Item
            "Duplicate"
        Item
            color #EF4444
            "Delete"

content.text: Use select for persistent selection that survives closing:

content.code:
Item:
    padding 12 16
    radius 6
    cursor pointer
    state default
        background transparent
    state highlighted
        background #333
    state selected
        background #2271c1
    onclick select self
    onhover highlight self

Menu
    vertical
    gap 2
    background #1A1A23
    padding 8
    radius 8
    width 200
    Item
        "Option A"
    Item
        "Option B"
    Item
        "Option C"

content.text: highlight is temporary (for navigation). select is persistent (for choosing).
Combining everything into a production-ready pattern with variable binding, hover/active states, and keyboard navigation:

content.code:
// Variable für den ausgewählten Wert
$selected: "Select..."

// Item-Templates für verschiedene Positionen
Dropdown.FirstItem: pad 8 12, rad tl 4 tr 4, cursor pointer
    onhover highlight self
    state highlighted bg #333
    state active bg #222

Dropdown.Item: pad 8 12, rad 0, cursor pointer
    onhover highlight self
    state highlighted bg #333
    state active bg #222

Dropdown.LastItem: pad 8 12, rad bl 4 br 4, cursor pointer
    onhover highlight self
    state highlighted bg #333
    state active bg #222

// Dropdown-Komponente
Dropdown
    state closed
    onclick-outside close
    Trigger hor, ver-cen, gap 8, pad 8 12, bg #252525, rad 6, bor 1 #444, hover-bg #333, cursor pointer
        onclick toggle
        Label $selected
        Icon "chevron-down", size 16
    Menu ver, bg #1E1E1E, rad 6, pad 4, min-width 180, focusable, clip
        if open
        onload focus
        keys
            escape close
            arrow-down highlight next
            arrow-up highlight prev
            enter select highlighted, close
        - Dropdown.FirstItem onclick assign $selected to "Option 1", close
            "Option 1"
        - Dropdown.Item onclick assign $selected to "Option 2", close
            "Option 2"
        - Dropdown.LastItem onclick assign $selected to "Option 3", close
            "Option 3"

content.text: Key features:

items: Variable binding: $selected stores the chosen value. The trigger displays $selected and updates on item click.
Hover effect: onhover highlight self activates the highlighted state with bg #333.
Active/click effect: state active bg #222 provides visual feedback during mousedown.
Rounded corners: FirstItem uses rad tl 4 tr 4 (top), LastItem uses rad bl 4 br 4 (bottom), middle items have rad 0.
Keyboard navigation: The keys block handles Escape, ArrowDown/Up, and Enter.

content.text: Simplified version (without position-specific corners):

content.code:
$selected: "Select..."

Dropdown.Item: pad 8 12, cursor pointer
    onhover highlight self
    state highlighted bg #333
    state active bg #222

Dropdown
    state closed
    onclick-outside close
    Trigger hor, ver-cen, gap 8, pad 8 12, bg #252525, rad 6, hover-bg #333, cursor pointer
        onclick toggle
        $selected
        Icon "chevron-down"
    Menu ver, bg #1E1E1E, rad 6, min-width 180, focusable, clip
        if open
        keys
            escape close
            arrow-down highlight next
            arrow-up highlight prev
        - Dropdown.Item onclick assign $selected to "Option 1", close
            "Option 1"
        - Dropdown.Item onclick assign $selected to "Option 2", close
            "Option 2"
        - Dropdown.Item onclick assign $selected to "Option 3", close
            "Option 3"

content.text: Both versions provide a polished dropdown with mouse and keyboard support. The behavior reads like documentation: "on keydown arrow-down, highlight next."
When interactions grow complex, separate structure from behavior with the events block:

content.code:
// === COMPONENTS ===
Input
    named Email "Email"
    padding 12
    radius 8
    background #1A1A23
    border 1 #333
Input
    named Password "Password"
    type password
    padding 12
    radius 8
    background #1A1A23
    border 1 #333
Error:
    color #EF4444
    font-size 13
Button
    named Submit bg #2271c1
    padding 12
    radius 8
    "Login"

$error: ""

// === LAYOUT ===
LoginForm
    vertical
    gap 12
    padding 24
    background #0F0F14
    radius 12
    width 280
    Email
    Password
    if $error
        Error
            $error
    Submit

// === BEHAVIOR ===
events
    Email
        onchange
        assign $error to ""
    Password
        onchange
        assign $error to ""
    Submit
        onclick
        if Email.value and Password.value
            assign $error to ""
            Submit
                .label = "Success!"
        else
            assign $error to "Please fill all fields"

content.text: Structure at the top, behavior at the bottom. Each section has one job. As forms grow, this separation keeps code maintainable.

content.task: Build a tab component: three tab buttons that highlight on hover and select on click. Use state highlighted and state selected.
content.code:
Tab: pad 12 24, cursor pointer
  state default
    bg transparent, bor b 2, boc transparent
  state highlighted
    boc #333
  state selected
    boc #2271c1
  onclick select self
  onhover highlight self

Tabs hor, g 4, bg #1A1A23, rad 8
  Tab "Overview"
  Tab "Details"
  Tab "Settings"

content.text: Behaviors are declarative. You describe what should happen, not how. Mirror handles event listeners, state management, focus handling, and cleanup automatically.

sections.title: Doc Mode
sections.lead: Mirror includes a documentation mode for creating formatted text with Markdown-like syntax. Perfect for tutorials, documentation pages, and content-heavy interfaces.

content.text: The text component renders formatted text blocks using a multiline string with Markdown-compatible syntax:

content.code:
text
    '# Welcome to Mirror

    $p This is a **paragraph** with _italic_ text.
    Click
        [here](https://example.com) for more.'

content.text: The text content is wrapped in single quotes. Line breaks inside the string create separate blocks.
Block elements start at the beginning of a line:

content.code:
text
    '# Getting Started

    $lead Mirror is a description language for UI.

    ## Installation

    $p Install from npm:

    $li Run npm install
    $li Import the library
    $li Start building'

content.text: Format text within paragraphs using these markers:

content.code:
text
    '$p Use **bold** for emphasis, _italic_ for terms,
    and `code` for technical content.

    $p Read the [documentation](https://docs.example.com) for details.'

content.text: To use literal asterisks or underscores, escape them with backslash: \* or \_
For live code examples in documentation, use the playground component. It shows Mirror code with syntax highlighting and a live preview:

content.code:
playground
    'Button bg #2271c1, pad 12 24, rad 8, "Click me"'

content.text: The playground is ideal for tutorials where readers can see both the code and its rendered result side-by-side.
Build complete documentation pages by combining text and playground components:

content.code:
Doc
    vertical
    gap 24
    padding 32
    max-width 800
    text
        '# Buttons

        $lead Buttons trigger actions when clicked.
